title: Lipstick on Silk
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (wax + oil + pigment/dye). Lipstick is a three-phase system: (1) WAX BASE — carnauba wax, beeswax, or synthetic wax providing structure, (2) OIL PHASE — castor oil, lanolin, or mineral oil as carrier and moisturizer, (3) PIGMENT/DYE — typically iron oxide (reds, pinks), titanium dioxide (lighter shades), and synthetic dyes like D&C Red. The treatment sequence is critical: scrape wax first, POG for the oil phase, then dye spotter for the pigment. Dan Eisen's procedure for lipstick: use Vaseline (petroleum jelly) as lubricant to loosen pigments, then detergent, then ammonia and peroxide. The IRTA report confirmed lipstick responds to POG agents on silk. On silk — a protein fiber — every step must respect the fiber's pH sensitivity and water-spotting tendency.
whyThisWorks: Treating in phase order prevents cross-contamination: wax removed mechanically first (prevents it from smearing), oil dissolved by POG second (clears the path to the pigment), pigment/dye addressed last. Each phase removal makes the next phase more accessible. On silk, the POG agent dissolves the oil carrier without water contact — critical because water creates ring marks on silk instantly. The dye spotter then has direct access to the pigment on the fiber surface.

spottingProtocol.technique: Physical removal of surface wax before chemical treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Gently scrape off as much surface wax as possible using a dull spatula or credit card edge. On silk, be extremely careful — the fiber is fragile and scratches show. Scrape in one direction only, lifting the spatula between strokes. Never rub back and forth. Remove every bit of solid wax you can see — any wax left will smear and spread when you apply solvent. Dan Eisen advises using Vaseline as a lubricant during lipstick removal — apply a thin layer to help the scraping action without abrading the silk.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull spatula or credit card, petroleum jelly (optional lubricant)

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or equivalent
spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve the oil/wax carrier — dryside treatment on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG agent to dissolve the remaining wax and oil carrier. Place stain face-down on clean towel, apply POG from the back to push dissolved lipstick OUT of the silk. Light tapping only — silk is weak when wet. The IRTA report specifically tested lipstick on silk with POG agents (Soy Gold 2500, DPM) and confirmed effectiveness. You should see color transferring to the towel. Rotate towel areas. Flush with VDS. Repeat until oil transfer stops — typically 2-3 passes. Don't move to dye treatment until the oil phase is fully addressed.
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, spotting brush (light pressure), VDS, clean white towels

spottingProtocol.technique: Target remaining dye/pigment — solvent for synthetic dye component
spottingProtocol.instruction: After the oil phase is removed, any remaining stain is pigment/dye. Apply amyl acetate (safe on silk) or isopropyl alcohol to dissolve the synthetic dye component. Work gently from edge to center. Flush with VDS. If the pigment is iron oxide-based (common in red/pink lipsticks), it may not dissolve in organic solvents — it's a mineral pigment. In that case, the color will respond to gentle acidic treatment in the wetside step.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate or isopropyl alcohol, spotting brush, VDS

spottingProtocol.agent: Neutral lubricant + gentle water flush + acetic acid
spottingProtocol.technique: Wetside pigment treatment — acid for mineral pigments on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: If pigment residue remains: apply neutral lubricant first (protects silk during water contact). Flush gently with water gun at safe distance. Apply dilute acetic acid (28% GAA at 1:10) — this helps break iron oxide pigment bonds. Dan Eisen: ammonia and hydrogen peroxide is the next escalation, but on silk, ammonia must be very dilute and brief — silk tolerates pH 4-7, not higher. Apply H2O2 3% (not 6%) with minimal ammonia, work quickly, flush immediately. On silk, contact time for any bleaching agent should be under 2 minutes.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Neutral lubricant, water gun, acetic acid (1:10), H2O2 3% (if needed), ammonia 28% (very dilute)

spottingProtocol.agent: Leveling agent — prevent water rings on silk
spottingProtocol.technique: Critical ring prevention — non-negotiable on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Spray leveling agent onto the entire treated area and extend 2-3 inches beyond the wet boundary. This prevents the water ring that would ruin the treatment on silk. Feather the edges carefully. On silk, this step is as important as the stain removal itself — a visible water ring is as objectionable as lipstick.

Air dry completely. No heat until verified clean. Inspect under strong angled light — silk's natural sheen makes any residual stain or ring visible. If pigment shadow remains, repeat from step 2. If clean, proceed to normal finishing with low heat press.

homeSolutions: Scrape off surface wax gently with a credit card or dull knife. Remove as much solid material as possible before any liquid treatment.

Apply a small amount of petroleum jelly (Vaseline) to the remaining stain — this sounds counterintuitive, but Eisen recommends it: the petroleum jelly softens the remaining wax and helps the pigment release. Gently work with the back of a spoon, blot with a clean white cloth.
Follow with a drop of mild dish soap (Dawn) mixed with cool water. Dab gently — NEVER rub silk. Blot with a clean pad.
Rinse the area with cool water mixed with a splash of white vinegar. Air dry flat. Take to a dry cleaner if any color remains — DIY bleaching on silk is extremely risky.
materialWarnings: Silk is a PROTEIN fiber — NEVER use protease enzymes. They digest silk.
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk — destroys protein structure, causes irreversible yellowing.
Keep pH between 4.0-7.0 on silk. Ammonia above pH 8 weakens fibroin.
Silk is weak when wet — minimal mechanical action. No aggressive rubbing or scrubbing.
Water-spotting on silk: leveling agent is mandatory after any wet treatment.
Hydrogen peroxide above 3% can weaken silk — use minimal concentration for minimal time.
Test colorfastness on hidden seam — silk dyes are notoriously unstable to solvents and water.
Iron oxide pigment in lipstick can actually stain silk more aggressively than the dye component — it's a mineral deposit.
escalation: If lipstick pigment persists after full protocol: the stain is likely iron oxide (mineral pigment) embedded in the silk. Oxalic acid can address iron oxide but is rated D (limited use) on silk — professional application only with very brief contact time and immediate neutralization. For extreme cases, reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) applied by Q-tip may address the dye component without destroying silk — but test first. On silk, know when to stop: aggressive escalation risks fiber damage that's worse than the remaining stain.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Dissolves the wax and oil carrier in lipstick. IRTA-tested effective on lipstick+silk.

professional.name: Amyl Acetate (Banana Oil)
professional.amazonSearchQuery: amyl acetate banana oil spotting solvent
professional.note: Targets synthetic dye component after oil removal. Safe on silk.

professional.name: Sofspot Leveling Agent
professional.note: Prevents water rings — mandatory on silk after any wet treatment.

household.name: Petroleum Jelly (Vaseline)
household.note: Dan Eisen's recommended lubricant for lipstick removal. Softens wax, releases pigment.

household.name: Dawn Dish Soap (Original Blue)
household.note: Mild surfactant for the oil phase. Use sparingly on silk with cool water.

customerExplanation: Lipstick is a combination of wax, oil, and pigment — three different types of stain in one mark. We treat each layer in order: first we remove the waxy surface, then dissolve the oil carrier, and finally address the color pigment. On silk, every step requires special care because it's a delicate protein fiber that can water-spot easily and doesn't tolerate harsh chemicals. The leveling step prevents any ring marks from the treatment.
commonMistakes: Rubbing the lipstick before scraping — this pushes wax deeper into the silk weave and spreads pigment across a larger area. Always scrape first. | Using water before solvent — water drives the oil phase deeper and creates ring marks on silk. Start dryside. | Aggressive bleaching on silk — hydrogen peroxide above 3% can weaken silk permanently. Less is more on this fiber. | Skipping the leveling agent on silk — leaves visible water rings.
scienceNote: Lipstick is classified as a combination stain in RR Street's system: it has both dry-side (wax, oil) and wet-side (some water-soluble dyes) components. The correct sequence is always dry-side first, then wet-side. Iron oxide — the primary pigment in red and pink lipsticks — is the same compound that makes rust stains. On silk, this creates a particular challenge because HF-based rust removers (Picrin) are contraindicated on protein fibers. The best approach on silk is gentle acidic treatment with acetic acid, which slowly chelates iron without the hazards of HF.
callPro: Lipstick on silk should go to a professional dry cleaner — the combination of wax, oil, and mineral pigment on a delicate protein fiber requires controlled solvency and precise mechanical technique that home methods can't replicate without risking water marks or fiber damage.
difficulty_factors: Three-phase stain (wax + oil + pigment) requires sequential treatment
Silk is delicate — weak when wet, water-spots easily, limited bleaching tolerance
Iron oxide pigment is a mineral deposit — resists organic solvents
pH must stay 4-7 throughout — silk intolerant of alkali
solventNote: For PERC shops: PERC on silk requires caution — may affect hand-feel and leave odor. GreenEarth or hydrocarbon preferred for silk. Pre-spot lipstick on board first — machine processing alone won't remove pigment. Level with Sofspot before loading.
